questions.question:
Let f(n) = n and g(n) = n^(1 - sin(n)), where n is a positive integer. Which of the following statements is/are correct?
I. f(n) = O(g(n))
II. f(n) = Omega(g(n))

A. Only I
B. Only II
C. Both I and II
D. Neither I nor II
questions.to_find: Which asymptotic relationships hold between f(n)=n and g(n)=n^(1-sin(n))

understand.plain: We have f(n) = n and g(n) = n^(1 - sin(n)). Since n is a positive integer, sin(n) oscillates between values close to -1 and +1 (but never exactly, since pi is irrational). This means the exponent (1 - sin(n)) oscillates between values near 0 and near 2, making g(n) oscillate between values close to n^0 = 1 and n^2. So f(n)/g(n) = n^(sin(n)) has no fixed bound in either direction — it neither stays bounded above nor stays bounded below by a positive constant.

keywords.explain: f(n) = O(g(n)) means there exist constants c > 0 and n_0 such that f(n) <= c * g(n) for all n >= n_0.
keywords.example: n = O(n^2) because n <= n^2 for large n.

keywords.explain: f(n) = Omega(g(n)) means there exist constants c > 0 and n_0 such that f(n) >= c * g(n) for all n >= n_0.
keywords.example: n^2 = Omega(n) because n^2 >= n for large n.

keywords.explain: For integer n (in radians), sin(n) is dense in [-1, 1] by the equidistribution theorem — it gets arbitrarily close to both 1 and -1.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='400' height='160' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='400' height='160' rx='10' class='bg'/><text x='200' y='28' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Exponent of g(n) = n^(1-sin(n))</text><text x='200' y='55' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>1 - sin(n) oscillates in (0, 2)</text><rect x='20' y='70' width='170' height='40' rx='6' class='hi'/><text x='105' y='86' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>sin(n) near +1</text><text x='105' y='104' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>g(n) ~ n^0 = 1 (tiny!)</text><rect x='210' y='70' width='170' height='40' rx='6' class='hi'/><text x='295' y='86' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>sin(n) near -1</text><text x='295' y='104' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>g(n) ~ n^2 (huge!)</text><text x='200' y='148' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>f(n)=n is neither O(g(n)) nor Omega(g(n))</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: The exponent of g(n) = n^(1-sin(n)) oscillates between near 0 and near 2, so g(n) itself oscillates between nearly 1 and nearly n^2, making f(n)=n sometimes greater and sometimes smaller than g(n).

given.aim: Determine which asymptotic order relations hold between f(n)=n and g(n)=n^(1-sin(n)).

terms.connects: The function whose order we are comparing.

terms.meaning: The exponent (1 - sin(n)) varies with n; since sin(n) oscillates in (-1,1) for integer n, the exponent varies in (0,2).
terms.example: If sin(n)~1, g(n)~n^0=1; if sin(n)~-1, g(n)~n^2.
terms.connects: The oscillating nature prevents any constant-factor bound between f and g.

terms.meaning: The ratio of f to g equals n^(sin(n)), which oscillates without a fixed upper or lower bound.
terms.example: When sin(n)~1, ratio ~ n (unbounded above). When sin(n)~-1, ratio ~ 1/n (unbounded below by positive constant).
terms.connects: Proves neither O nor Omega holds.

given.plan: Compute f(n)/g(n) = n / n^(1-sin(n)) = n^(sin(n)). Check if this ratio stays bounded above (O relationship) or bounded below by a positive constant (Omega relationship) for all sufficiently large n. Because sin(n) is dense in (-1,1) for integer n, the ratio is unbounded both ways.

steps.title: Simplify the ratio f(n)/g(n)
steps.apply: f(n)/g(n) = n^(sin(n)). For f(n)=O(g(n)), we need n^(sin(n)) <= c for all large n. For f(n)=Omega(g(n)), we need n^(sin(n)) >= c > 0 for all large n.
steps.note: The key is the behaviour of sin(n) for positive integer n.

steps.title: Check O: is n^(sin(n)) bounded above?
steps.formula_raw: f(n) = O(g(n)) iff exists c, n_0: n^(sin(n)) <= c for all n >= n_0
steps.apply: By the equidistribution theorem, {n mod 2*pi} is dense in [0, 2*pi] for integer n. So sin(n) gets arbitrarily close to 1. Hence n^(sin(n)) gets arbitrarily close to n, which is unbounded. So f(n) != O(g(n)).

steps.title: Check Omega: is n^(sin(n)) bounded below by a positive constant?
steps.formula_raw: f(n) = Omega(g(n)) iff exists c > 0, n_0: n^(sin(n)) >= c for all n >= n_0
steps.apply: Similarly, sin(n) gets arbitrarily close to -1 for integer n, so n^(sin(n)) gets arbitrarily close to n^(-1) = 1/n -> 0. So the ratio is not bounded below by any positive constant. Hence f(n) != Omega(g(n)).
steps.note: Statement II is also FALSE. Answer is D.

solution.result: Neither Statement I nor Statement II is correct. The answer is D.

questions.formula_note: Key idea: equidistribution of {n mod 2*pi} in [0,2*pi] for integer n implies sin(n) is dense in (-1,1), making n^(sin(n)) oscillate unboundedly in both directions.

questions.question:
Let f(n), g(n) and h(n) be functions defined for positive integers such that f(n) = Omega(g(n)), g(n) = Omega(h(n)), g(n) != O(f(n)), and h(n) != O(f(n)). Which one of the following statements is FALSE?

A. f(n) = Omega(h(n))
B. h(n) = O(f(n))
C. f(n) != O(g(n))
D. f(n)(h(n)) != O(g(n)(h(n)))
questions.to_find: Which statement is FALSE given the asymptotic conditions on f, g, h

understand.plain: We are given relationships between three functions f, g, h using big-Omega notation, with some explicit inequalities. We need to determine which of the four given statements does NOT follow from (or contradicts) the given conditions. Statement B claims h(n) = O(f(n)), but the problem explicitly states h(n) != O(f(n)), so B is directly contradicted.

keywords.explain: f = Omega(g) means f grows at least as fast as g (g is a lower bound for f).
keywords.example: n^2 = Omega(n) since n^2 >= 1*n for large n.

keywords.explain: If f = Omega(g) and g = Omega(h), then f = Omega(h) by transitivity.
keywords.example: n^3 = Omega(n^2) and n^2 = Omega(n), so n^3 = Omega(n).

understand.visual_alt: Given f grows at least as fast as g, and g grows at least as fast as h, but g does not grow as slowly as f (strict dominance), and h does not grow as slowly as f.

given.aim: Identify the FALSE statement among the four options given the conditions on f, g, h.

terms.meaning: f grows at least as fast as g; equivalently g = O(f).
terms.example: f ~ n^2 log n, g ~ n^2.
terms.connects: This tells us f is at least as large as g asymptotically.

terms.meaning: g grows at least as fast as h.
terms.connects: By transitivity, f = Omega(h) must also hold.

terms.meaning: g grows strictly faster than f; equivalently f grows strictly slower than g. Similarly h is not O(f).
terms.example: If f ~ n^2 log n, g ~ n^2 does NOT satisfy g != O(f). Need f strictly slower than g.
terms.connects: This explicitly tells us h(n) is NOT O(f(n)), directly making option B FALSE.

given.plan: Check each option against the given conditions: A uses transitivity (should be true), B directly contradicts h(n) != O(f(n)) (so B is false), C follows from g != O(f) (i.e., f != O(g)), D multiplication preserves the strict order.

steps.title: Check option A: f(n) = Omega(h(n))
steps.formula_raw: f = Omega(g) and g = Omega(h) => f = Omega(h) by transitivity
steps.apply: Since f = Omega(g) and g = Omega(h), by transitivity of Omega, f = Omega(h). Statement A is TRUE.
steps.note: Transitivity is a fundamental property of asymptotic notations.

steps.title: Check option B: h(n) = O(f(n))
steps.apply: The problem explicitly states h(n) != O(f(n)). Statement B directly contradicts this given condition, so B is FALSE.
steps.note: This is the answer — B is the FALSE statement.

steps.title: Verify options C and D are true
steps.formula_raw: g != O(f) <=> f != O(g) is not necessarily equivalent, but given g != O(f) and f = Omega(g) with g != O(f) implies f does not grow as fast as g
steps.apply: C: f != O(g) follows because if f = O(g) and f = Omega(g) then f = Theta(g) which would mean g = O(f), contradicting g != O(f). So C is TRUE. D: Since f is not O(g), multiplying both sides by h preserves the strict ordering, so f*h != O(g*h). D is TRUE.
steps.note: B is the unique FALSE statement.

solution.result: Statement B (h(n) = O(f(n))) is FALSE because the problem explicitly states h(n) != O(f(n)).

questions.formula_note: Transitivity of asymptotic notations: if f = Omega(g) and g = Omega(h) then f = Omega(h). Also, given conditions directly state h(n) != O(f(n)), making option B trivially false.

questions.question:
Arrange the following functions in increasing asymptotic order:
a. n^(1/3)
b. e^n
c. n^(7/4)
d. n^(log_2(n))
e. 1.0000001^n

A. a, d, c, e, b
B. d, a, c, e, b
C. a, c, d, e, b
D. a, c, d, b, e
questions.answer: C. a, c, d, e, b
questions.to_find: The increasing asymptotic order of the five given functions

understand.plain: We need to sort five functions from slowest-growing to fastest-growing. Polynomial functions (n^k) grow slower than exponential functions. Among polynomials, compare exponents. n^(log_2(n)) is superpolynomial (its exponent grows) but subexponential. Among exponentials, compare the base: 1.0000001^n grows much slower than e^n (since e ~ 2.718 >> 1.0000001).

keywords.explain: A function that grows faster than any fixed-exponent polynomial but slower than any exponential with base > 1 is called superpolynomial subexponential. n^(log n) is such a function.
keywords.example: n^(log_2(n)) at n=1024: 1024^10 = 10^30, while 2^1024 is astronomically larger.

keywords.explain: For a^n vs b^n with a > b > 1, a^n grows faster. e ~ 2.718, so e^n >> 1.0000001^n.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='420' height='120' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='420' height='120' rx='10' class='bg'/><text x='210' y='25' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Increasing Asymptotic Order</text><text x='15' y='55' class='sub ink'>Slow</text><text x='365' y='55' class='sub ink'>Fast</text><line x1='40' y1='60' x2='390' y2='60' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><text x='50' y='90' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>a</text><text x='50' y='105' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>n^(1/3)</text><text x='130' y='90' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>c</text><text x='130' y='105' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>n^(7/4)</text><text x='210' y='90' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>d</text><text x='210' y='105' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>n^(lgn)</text><text x='295' y='90' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>e</text><text x='295' y='105' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>1.0000001^n</text><text x='375' y='90' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>b</text><text x='375' y='105' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>e^n</text><circle cx='50' cy='60' r='5' class='ok'/><circle cx='130' cy='60' r='5' class='ok'/><circle cx='210' cy='60' r='5' class='ok'/><circle cx='295' cy='60' r='5' class='ok'/><circle cx='375' cy='60' r='5' class='ok'/></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A left-to-right ordering from slowest to fastest: n^(1/3) < n^(7/4) < n^(log_2 n) < 1.0000001^n < e^n.

given.aim: Sort the five functions by their asymptotic growth rate from smallest to largest.

terms.meaning: a = n^(1/3) has exponent 1/3; c = n^(7/4) has exponent 1.75. Since 1/3 < 7/4, we have n^(1/3) << n^(7/4).
terms.connects: Both are standard polynomials, ordered by exponent.

terms.meaning: The exponent log_2(n) itself grows, so this grows faster than any fixed polynomial but slower than any exponential (base > 1).
terms.connects: d sits between polynomials and exponentials.

terms.meaning: e = 1.0000001^n and b = e^n. Since e ~ 2.718 >> 1.0000001, b grows vastly faster than e.
terms.example: At n=10^7: e=(1.0000001)^(10^7) ~ e^1 ~ 2.718, while b=e^(10^7) is astronomically larger.
terms.connects: Both are exponential; base e >> 1.0000001 makes e^n the fastest.

given.plan: First separate polynomials (a, c) from superpolynomial (d) from exponentials (e, b). Order polynomials by exponent. Place d between poly and exponential. Order exponentials by their base.

steps.title: Order the polynomial functions
steps.note: For polynomials n^k, smaller k means slower growth.

steps.title: Place n^(log_2(n)) relative to polynomials
steps.formula_raw: n^(log_2(n)) vs n^k: log_2(n) eventually exceeds any constant k
steps.apply: For any fixed constant exponent k, once n > 2^k, we have log_2(n) > k, so n^(log_2(n)) > n^k. Hence n^(7/4) = o(n^(log_2(n))). Order: a < c < d.
steps.note: n^(log n) is superpolynomial — it surpasses all polynomials eventually.

steps.title: Compare exponentials with n^(log_2(n))
steps.formula_raw: Any a^n (a>1) vs n^(log n): exponentials dominate superpolynomial
steps.apply: Even 1.0000001^n grows faster than n^(log_2(n)) for sufficiently large n (exponentials dominate all polynomial-type functions). So: a < c < d < e.
steps.note: This is a standard fact: exponential beats any function of the form n^(f(n)) if f(n) = o(n/log n).

steps.title: Order the two exponentials
steps.note: This matches option C: a, c, d, e, b.

solution.result: The increasing asymptotic order is a, c, d, e, b. Answer is C.

questions.formula_note: Hierarchy of growth rates: polynomial (n^k, ordered by k) < superpolynomial (n^(log n)) < any exponential (a^n, a>1, ordered by base). Among exponentials, larger base grows faster.

questions.question:
Which of the following statement(s) is/are correct regarding Bellman-Ford shortest path algorithm?
P: Always finds a negative weighted cycle, if one exists.
Q: Finds whether any negative weighted cycle is reachable from the source.

A. Only P
B. Only Q
C. Both P and Q
D. Neither P nor Q
questions.to_find: Which of the two statements about Bellman-Ford algorithm are correct

understand.plain: Bellman-Ford is a single-source shortest path algorithm that can detect negative weight cycles. Statement P says it always finds a negative cycle if one exists anywhere in the graph. Statement Q says it finds whether a negative cycle is reachable from the source. The key distinction is: Bellman-Ford only considers paths reachable from the source — it cannot detect negative cycles that are not reachable from the source vertex.

keywords.explain: A shortest path algorithm that relaxes all edges (V-1) times, then checks for negative cycles by doing one more relaxation — if any distance decreases, a negative cycle reachable from source exists.
keywords.example: On a graph with 4 vertices, relax all edges 3 times, then check once more for further relaxation.

keywords.explain: A cycle in a weighted graph where the sum of edge weights is negative. Shortest paths are undefined when such a cycle is reachable from the source.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='400' height='160' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='400' height='160' rx='10' class='bg'/><circle cx='60' cy='80' r='22' class='hi'/><text x='60' y='85' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>S</text><circle cx='180' cy='40' r='22' class='bg'/><text x='180' y='45' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>A</text><circle cx='300' cy='40' r='22' class='hi'/><text x='300' y='45' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>B</text><line x1='82' y1='72' x2='158' y2='48' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2'/><path d='M278,40 Q290,15 315,28 Q328,40 315,52 Q290,65 278,52 Z' fill='none' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='180' cy='130' r='22' class='bg'/><text x='180' y='135' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>C</text><circle cx='300' cy='130' r='22' class='hi'/><text x='300' y='135' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>D</text><path d='M258,130 Q280,108 302,120 Q314,130 302,142 Q280,152 258,142 Z' fill='none' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='2'/><text x='330' y='45' class='sub no'>neg cycle</text><text x='330' y='135' class='sub mut'>unreachable</text><text x='180' y='155' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>B-F detects only reachable neg cycles</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A graph with source S connected to A and B. B has a negative cycle (detected by Bellman-Ford). D also has a negative cycle but is not reachable from S (not detected by Bellman-Ford).

given.aim: Determine which statement(s) correctly describe the behaviour of Bellman-Ford regarding negative cycles.

terms.meaning: Bellman-Ford always finds any negative cycle in the graph, even if unreachable from source.
terms.example: A disconnected negative cycle in a separate component.
terms.connects: This is FALSE — Bellman-Ford only considers paths from the source.

terms.meaning: Bellman-Ford detects negative cycles reachable from the source.
terms.example: After V-1 relaxations, if one more relaxation further reduces a distance, there is a negative cycle reachable from source.
terms.connects: This is TRUE — exactly what Bellman-Ford guarantees.

terms.meaning: Bellman-Ford only updates distances for vertices reachable from the source. Unreachable parts of the graph are unaffected.
terms.example: If negative cycle is in a separate component, distances there are never updated.
terms.connects: This is why P is false and Q is the precise correct statement.

given.plan: Recall Bellman-Ford: relax all edges V-1 times, then do one more pass. If any distance decreases, there is a negative cycle reachable from source. It cannot detect unreachable cycles, so P is false and Q is true.

steps.title: Evaluate Statement P
steps.formula_raw: Bellman-Ford detects negative cycles via the (V)th relaxation pass
steps.apply: Bellman-Ford operates from a source and only relaxes paths reachable from that source. If a negative cycle exists in a part of the graph not reachable from the source, Bellman-Ford will not detect it. Statement P claims it 'always finds a negative weighted cycle, if one exists' — this is too strong. P is FALSE.
steps.note: Bellman-Ford is source-centric; it cannot see unreachable components.

steps.title: Evaluate Statement Q
steps.formula_raw: If d[v] can be decreased after V-1 iterations => negative cycle reachable from source
steps.apply: Statement Q says Bellman-Ford finds whether any negative cycle is reachable from the source. This is exactly what the algorithm detects in its Vth relaxation pass. Q is TRUE.
steps.note: This is the standard use of Bellman-Ford for negative cycle detection.

solution.result: Only Q is correct. The answer is B.

questions.formula_note: Bellman-Ford detects negative cycles reachable from source by performing V relaxation passes total (V-1 for shortest paths + 1 to check for negative cycles). It cannot detect cycles unreachable from the source.

questions.question:
What is the time complexity of Bellman-Ford single-source shortest path algorithm on a complete graph of n vertices?

A. Theta(n^2)
B. Theta(n^2 log n)
C. Theta(n^3)
D. Theta(n^3 log n)
questions.to_find: Time complexity of Bellman-Ford on a complete graph of n vertices

understand.plain: Bellman-Ford runs in O(V * E) time where V is the number of vertices and E is the number of edges. A complete graph of n vertices has n*(n-1)/2 undirected edges, or n*(n-1) directed edges. Substituting: Theta(n * n^2) = Theta(n^3). The standard complete graph in algorithms context has Theta(n^2) edges.

keywords.meaning: A graph where every pair of distinct vertices is connected by an edge. Has n*(n-1)/2 undirected edges (or n*(n-1) directed edges).

keywords.explain: Runs (V-1) relaxation passes, each pass relaxes all E edges. Total: O(V*E).
keywords.example: For V=5 vertices and E=10 edges: 4*10=40 operations.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='360' height='140' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='360' height='140' rx='10' class='bg'/><text x='180' y='22' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Bellman-Ford on K_n</text><rect x='20' y='35' width='150' height='40' rx='6' class='hi'/><text x='95' y='52' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>V = n vertices</text><text x='95' y='70' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Passes = (n-1) = Theta(n)</text><rect x='190' y='35' width='150' height='40' rx='6' class='hi'/><text x='265' y='52' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>E = n(n-1) edges</text><text x='265' y='70' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Directed: Theta(n^2)</text><text x='180' y='100' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Time = O(V * E)</text><text x='180' y='125' text-anchor='middle' class='big ink'>= Theta(n * n^2) = Theta(n^3)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Bellman-Ford time = O(V * E). For K_n: V = n, E = Theta(n^2), so total = Theta(n^3).

given.aim: Compute Bellman-Ford time complexity for a complete graph of n vertices.

terms.meaning: Number of vertices in the graph.
terms.connects: Bellman-Ford performs V-1 = n-1 main relaxation passes.

terms.meaning: Complete graph edge count. In either case E = Theta(n^2).
terms.connects: Each relaxation pass processes all E edges.

terms.meaning: The algorithm does V-1 passes, each looking at all E edges.

given.plan: Apply Bellman-Ford time complexity formula O(V*E). For a complete graph: V=n, E=Theta(n^2). Multiply to get Theta(n^3).

steps.title: Recall Bellman-Ford time complexity
steps.apply: Bellman-Ford performs (V-1) relaxation passes over all E edges. Time complexity = O(V * E).
steps.note: This holds for directed graphs. For undirected, each undirected edge is treated as 2 directed edges but remains Theta(V*E).

steps.title: Count edges in a complete graph of n vertices
steps.apply: For a complete directed graph: E = n*(n-1) = Theta(n^2). For undirected: E = n*(n-1)/2 = Theta(n^2). In both cases E = Theta(n^2).
steps.note: The exact constant does not affect the Theta notation.

steps.title: Compute total time complexity
steps.formula_raw: T = Theta(V * E) = Theta(n * n^2) = Theta(n^3)
steps.apply: V = n, E = Theta(n^2), so T = Theta(n * n^2) = Theta(n^3).

solution.result: Time complexity of Bellman-Ford on a complete graph of n vertices is Theta(n^3). Answer is C.

questions.formula_note: Bellman-Ford time complexity: O(V * E). For complete graph K_n: E = Theta(n^2), so total = Theta(n^3).
